subject.title: Mathematics Analysis and Approaches (AA)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics Analysis and Approaches** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **number and algebra, functions, geometry and trigonometry, statistics and probability, and calculus**, with a strong focus on **theoretical understanding, formal mathematical reasoning, and analytical problem-solving**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Non-calculator written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 2** | Calculator allowed written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 3** | Calculator allowed problem-solving | N/A | 60 minutes | 20% (HL only) |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal Assessment | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: Research question is clearly stated, focused on a specific modelling technique and outcome.
Title and introductory communication provide an effective overview of the investigationâs purpose.
Methodology is transparent, with VBA code included and clear definition of variables.
weaknesses: Introductory exposition occasionally drifts into generic textbook detail rather than staying focused on airport delays.
Justification of simulation parameters is brief and realâworld data were not obtained.
Conclusion section repeats earlier points rather than synthesizing them directly in relation to the research question.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand assessments place the work solidly in the 3â4 band (adequate communication, clear research question, mostly complete methodology).  However, occasional loss of focus on the airport context and only partial evidence of informed selection of data sources prevent a higher score.  A bandâleading mark of 4 is not merited, so the final holistic score is 3.

strengths: Consistent use of subject-specific terminology (e.g. funciÃ³n objetivo, variable de holgura) with generally correct application.
Accurate explanation of slack variable demonstration and simplex pivot steps in the appendix.
weaknesses: Application of knowledge to the airport context is limited to inserting simulated numbers without validation or sensitivity analysis.
Key mathematical formulations (e.g. inequality for track occupancy) are omitted in places, reducing completeness and rigor.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Knowledge of the simplex method is demonstrated in reproduction of textbook explanations, but realâworld application is limited and important formulations are sometimes missing.  This aligns with the upper end of the 1â2 band for subject knowledge and the lower end of the 3â4 band for terminology; overall a score of 2 out of 6 reflects limited yet partly relevant application.

strengths: Use of multiple sources (IBM, Skybrary, research articles) to provide model background.
Acknowledgement of theoretical vs. practical limits in the conclusion shows some critical awareness.
weaknesses: Analysis consists mainly of reporting Solverâs zeroâdelay result without mathematical justification, sensitivity checks or alternative comparisons.
Discussion of model limitations and future work remains descriptive and non-critical.
No deeper evaluation of assumptions, alternative methods or robustness beyond brief suggestions.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Though there is some justification of constraints with external sources and a descriptive discussion of limitations, the work lacks supported analysis, systematic evaluation and critical argumentation.  Two of three strands fall in the lowest band; a minimal score of 1 out of 12 most accurately reflects the overall weakness in critical thinking.

strengths: Overall structure is clear, with logical sections, numbered headings, table of contents and appendices.
Use of figures (graphical method) and tables (unit conversions, solver configuration) aids reader comprehension.
weaknesses: Page numbering is inconsistent or missing in places, and some tables overflow the page margins.
Equation formatting is occasionally inconsistent and inline math errors reduce readability.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The report follows a recognisable extended-essay structure and uses visual aids effectively, meriting placement in the 3â4 band for structure.  However, layout and formatting issues (missing page numbers, table overflow, inconsistent equation styling) remain and place layout in the 1â2 band.  A composite score of 2 out of 4 balances these strengths and weaknesses.

strandSelections.reasoning: The research questionââÂ¿Hasta quÃ© punto es posible utilizar un modelo de optimizaciÃ³n lineal multivariable para minimizar los retrasos â¦?ââis explicitly stated, focused on one modelling technique and one measurable outcome.  Sections of the essay repeatedly reference this question, showing connection to subsequent discussion.  Minor breadth remains (all airports, any delays), so perfect focus is not achieved, but clarity is strong enough for the top of the 3-4 band.

Analysis is limited to feeding the constraints into Solver and reporting a zero-delay outcome; no mathematical justification, sensitivity analysis or comparison with alternative models is pursued.  Conclusions are asserted rather than systematically supported, matching âlimited/partially relevant analysisâ in the 1-3 band.

Terminology such as âfunciÃ³n objetivoâ, âvariables de decisiÃ³nâ, ârestriccionesâ, âSimplexâ, âvariable de holguraâ is used regularly and generally correctly.  A few slips (e.g. mis-spelling or confusion between Î and t) appear, but do not impede understanding.  This corresponds to âmostly accurateâ use, placing the work in the 3-4 band.

A table of contents, tables, equations and images are present and generally readable; however, page numbers are missing in places, some tables overflow, and equation formatting is occasionally inconsistent.  These shortcomings place layout in the 1-2 band, at the upper score of 2.

The student reproduces textbook explanations of the Simplex method and demonstrates it on a copied example.  Application to the airport context is limited to inserting simulated numbers into Solver; no derivations, sensitivity checks or validation are carried out.  Knowledge is therefore âlimitedâ and relevance only partial, aligning with the upper end of the 1-2 band.

The student states the airport-delay context, explains optimisation in general terms and signposts the structure through a table of contents.  While the explanation is readable and purposeful, it drifts into lengthy textbook exposition and occasionally loses focus on the specific airport problem.  Overall the communication is clear enough to be âadequateâ, but not consistently concise or sharply aligned to the enquiry, fitting the 3-4 band.

The report follows a recognisable EE structure with title page, abstract-style introduction, numbered sections, appendices and references.  Headings and sub-headings help understanding and the order is logical, satisfying the criteria for âclear and appropriate structureâ (3-4 band).

The essay cites around a dozen sources (IBM, Skybrary, research articles) and uses them to justify constraints or background facts.  Data, however, are entirely simulated and only loosely linked to the question.  Relevance is therefore âmostlyâ rather than âconsistentlyâ assured, warranting the bottom of the 4-6 band.

The student does comment on limitations (simulated data, linearity, small sample size) and suggests future work, but the argument remains basic and largely descriptive.  Critical evaluation of assumptions or robustness is minimal, situating the work within the 1-3 band for discussion.

Methodology is described (definition of variables, generation of simulated data, use of Excel Solver, reference to the Simplex algorithm).  Evidence of informed selection is partial: real-world data are not obtained and justification of simulation parameters is brief.  A modest range of sources and methods is nevertheless present, matching the description of âmostly complete methodologyâ in the 3-4 band.

annotations.comment: The title clearly communicates the investigationâs focus and aligns well with the research question, providing an effective overview of the studyâs purpose.
The inclusion of the word count at 3982 meets the IB formatting requirement and demonstrates attention to submission guidelines.
The introductory paragraph provides general definitions of optimization but drifts away from the airport context; consider tightening the focus to link directly to airport delays.
The statistic from the Agencia Federal de AviaciÃ³n Civil (21.5% delays) anchors the problem in real data and strengthens relevance.
The research question is clearly stated and wellâintegrated here, but you could remind the reader of its scope more succinctly after the exposition.
The explanation of the components of an optimization model (objective, variables, constraints) is accurate and consistently uses correct terminology.
This section relies heavily on IBMâs generic definition without critically comparing alternative sources or methods; consider evaluating differences between sources.
In heading â2.1 â QuÃ© es la optimizaciÃ³n?â, the inline math âiâ appears as a formatting error; correct LaTeX or plain text usage to improve clarity.
The graphical method figures are well captioned and attributed, aiding comprehension of why this method is unsuitable for multivariable airport scheduling.
Definition of variable d_i and objective Z = â d_i is precise and effectively links decision variables to the research question.
The discussion of Lagrange and simplex methods lacks explicit contrast of computational complexity; include a brief comparison to justify your choice.
Explanation of slack variable demonstration is clear; examples using both â¤ and â¥ are a good pedagogical approach.
The conversion from inequality to equality for â¥ is explained textually but the second row of Table 2.3.3 lacks the final equation; include it for completeness.
When defining R3 (track occupancy), the actual mathematical formulation is omitted in the table; insert the explicit inequality to complete the model.
The tabular summary of the complete optimisation problem (EcuaciÃ³n 3.3.4) supports clarity and aids reader understanding.
Justification for using simulated data is good, but the rationale for limiting to five flights over 15 minutes is too narrow; discuss implications for generalizability.
The unit conversion table from minutes and seconds to hours is well structured and facilitates the simplex matrix assembly.
In the problem statement on page 17 the inline sequence of constraints becomes difficult to parse; consider listing each R1âR3 separately for readability.
The solver output table showing zero delays is clear, but a brief explanation of solver convergence criteria would enhance the critical analysis.
The conclusion acknowledges that zero delay is theoretically correct but unattainable in practice; an excellent critical evaluation of model limitations.
Conclusion section repeats limitations already discussed; consider synthesizing these points more concisely and linking them directly to the research question.
The appendix simplex example includes detailed pivot steps, demonstrating strong procedural understanding of the method.
The matrix operations in the Simplex demonstration are lengthy; consider summarising or highlighting only key iterations to improve readability and focus.
The VBA code for data simulation is fully included, demonstrating methodological transparency; citing reproducibility steps would further strengthen methodology.
The explanation of randomization parameters (30â45 s, separation choices) is clear; consider discussing how different distributions might affect model robustness.
The Excel solver configuration screenshot clearly maps cell ranges to variables, objective, and constraints, aiding reproducibility.
The reference list is comprehensive, demonstrating wide background reading; ensure consistent formatting of URLs and author names.
